name: श्रद्धात्रयविभागयोग
teaching: Your faith is not what you profess — it is what you are: 'a person is made of their śraddhā.' Food, worship, austerity, giving — each splits three ways by the strand behind it, and the same outward act can purify or poison depending on its spirit. The chapter ends with oṁ tat sat: the seal of the real on anything done with faith, and the verdict — asat, nothing — on anything done without it. Faith is not one thing but three, and a person is made of the faith they keep. Food, worship, discipline, and giving each carry the maker's grade — and three syllables, OM TAT SAT, re-address imperfect acts to the Real.
questions: Watch one ordinary choice today — a meal, a gift, a discipline. Which of the three faiths chose it?
Where do you practice austerity for the sake of being seen?
What in your life is done entirely without faith — and is it, as the chapter says, nothing?

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
ये शास्त्रविधिमुत्सृज्य यजन्ते श्रद्धयान्विताः ।
तेषां निष्ठा तु का कृष्ण सत्त्वमाहो रजस्तमः॥
verses.transliteration:
ye śāstravidhimutsṛjya yajante śraddhayānvitāḥ
teṣāṃ niṣṭhā tu kā kṛṣṇa sattvamāho rajastamaḥ
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: those who set aside the ordinances of scripture yet sacrifice filled with faith — what is their standing, Krishna? Sattva, rajas, or tamas?
verses.translation: Those who, setting aside the ordinances of the scriptures, perform sacrifice with faith, what is their condition, O Krishna? Is is Sattva, Rajas or Tamas?

anvaya.meaning: those who, setting aside scripture's rule
what then is their standing, O Krishna

verses.explanation: Arjuna catches the loose thread of 16.24 at once: scripture is the measure — but what of the millions who worship without ever reading it, full of śraddhā, sincere faith? The grandmother at the village shrine, the devotee of unlettered love. His question is generous and precise, and it opens the Gita's great analysis of faith itself.
verses.connection: Born straight from 16.23–24: where does heartfelt faith stand when the rulebook is absent?
verses.reflection: Honor the unread worshipers in your world today — faith without footnotes still reaches.
verses.background: Arjuna catches the loose thread of the last verse: but what of those who set scripture aside yet sacrifice with faith — what is their standing, O Krishna: sattva, rajas, or tamas?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
त्रिविधा भवति श्रद्धा देहिनां सा स्वभावजा ।
सात्त्विकी राजसी चैव तामसी चेति तां शृणु॥
verses.transliteration:
trividhā bhavati śraddhā dehināṃ sā svabhāvajā
sāttvikī rājasī caiva tāmasī ceti tāṃ śṛṇu
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: threefold is the faith of the embodied, born of their innate nature — sattvic, rajasic, and tamasic. Hear of it.
verses.translation: Threefold is the faith of the embodied, which is inherent in their nature the Sattvic (pure), the Rajasic (passionate) and the Tamasic (dark). Do thou hear of it.

anvaya.meaning: of the embodied — born of their own nature

verses.explanation: The answer reframes the question: faith is not one thing that people have or lack — it is svabhāva-jā, grown from one's own nature, and it comes in the three colors of everything else. No one is faithless; everyone's trust simply flows along the channel their being has cut. The real question is never *whether* you believe, but what quality of you is doing the believing.
verses.connection: Dissolves the believer/non-believer binary: faith universal, its grade variable — the chapter's thesis.
verses.reflection: Stop asking whether people believe today; ask instead what quality of them is doing the believing.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the chapter's psychology: faith itself is threefold, born of one's inner nature — a person is made of their faith; as one's faith is, so is one. The sattvic worship gods; the rajasic, spirits of power; the tamasic, ghosts and shades — and those who practice fierce austerity unordained by scripture, torturing the body's elements and Me within it, know them of demonic resolve.

verses.devanagari:
सत्त्वानुरूपा सर्वस्य श्रद्धा भवति भारत ।
श्रद्धामयोऽयं पुरुषो यो यच्छ्रद्धः स एव सः॥
verses.transliteration:
sattvānurūpā sarvasya śraddhā bhavati bhārata
śraddhāmayo'yaṃ puruṣo yo yacchraddhaḥ sa eva saḥ
verses.essence: The faith of each is according to their essential nature, Bharata. A person is made of faith: whatever one's faith is, that very thing one is.
verses.translation: The faith of each is in accordance with his nature, O Arjuna. The man consists of his faith; as a man's faith is, so is he.

anvaya.meaning: conforming to the inner being, of everyone
made of faith is this person
whatever one's faith — that very thing one is

verses.explanation: One of the Gita's most quoted axioms: śraddhāmayo 'yaṁ puruṣaḥ — a human being is *composed* of trust. We are built around what we bank on: whatever you rely upon when no one is watching — money, status, method, God — that reliance is your actual religion and your actual self. Yo yac-chraddhaḥ sa eva saḥ: show me your faith and I have met you. Sattvanurupa sarvasya shraddha — faith conforms to one's inner stuff, and then the axiom: shraddhamayo 'yam purusho — this person is made of faith; yo yach-chhraddhah sa eva sah, as one's trust is, so one is. The commentaries treat the verse as the deepest sentence of spiritual psychology in the poem: identity located not in beliefs professed but in reliance practiced. The transformative corollary the teachers draw: re-place the trust, and the self re-forms around it.
verses.connection: The chapter's center of gravity: identity defined by the direction of trust.
verses.schools_note: Commentators treat this as a hinge of spiritual psychology: Shankara reads 'sattva' here as the inner disposition shaped by past karma; later teachers extend it forward — since one *is* one's faith, deliberately re-placing one's trust is the deepest possible self-transformation.
verses.reflection: Run the axiom on yourself tonight: what I rely on when no one watches — that is my faith, and my self.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the chapter's psychology: faith itself is threefold, born of one's inner nature — a person is made of their faith; as one's faith is, so is one. The sattvic worship gods; the rajasic, spirits of power; the tamasic, ghosts and shades — and those who practice fierce austerity unordained by scripture, torturing the body's elements and Me within it, know them of demonic resolve.

verses.devanagari:
यजन्ते सात्त्विका देवान्यक्षरक्षांसि राजसाः ।
प्रेतान्भूतगणांश्चान्ये यजन्ते तामसा जनाः॥
verses.transliteration:
yajante sāttvikā devānyakṣarakṣāṃsi rājasāḥ
pretānbhūtagaṇāṃścānye yajante tāmasā janāḥ
verses.essence: Sattvic people worship the gods; the rajasic worship yakshas and rakshasas; the others — tamasic people — worship ghosts and hosts of spirits.
verses.translation: The Sattvic or the pure men worship the gods; the Rajasic or the passionate worship the Yakshas and the Rakshasas; the others (the Tamasic or the deluded people) worship the ghosts and the hosts of the nature-spirits.

anvaya.meaning: the sattvic sacrifice to the gods
to yakshas and rakshasas, the rajasic
to the departed and ghost-hosts, the others

verses.explanation: The first diagnostic: read a person's altar. Clarity bows to the luminous; restless ambition courts powers that grant and threaten — yakshas of wealth, rakshasas of force; darkness gravitates to the shadowy and the dead. Worship is a mirror before it is a door: each nature kneels to a magnified image of itself.
verses.connection: First application of 17.3: the chosen deity as a portrait of the chooser.
verses.reflection: Read one altar today — yours or another's — as a mirror of the worshiper.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the chapter's psychology: faith itself is threefold, born of one's inner nature — a person is made of their faith; as one's faith is, so is one. The sattvic worship gods; the rajasic, spirits of power; the tamasic, ghosts and shades — and those who practice fierce austerity unordained by scripture, torturing the body's elements and Me within it, know them of demonic resolve.

verses.devanagari:
अशास्त्रविहितं घोरं तप्यन्ते ये तपो जनाः ।
दम्भाहंकारसंयुक्ताः कामरागबलान्विताः॥
verses.transliteration:
aśāstravihitaṃ ghoraṃ tapyante ye tapo janāḥ
dambhāhaṃkārasaṃyuktāḥ kāmarāgabalānvitāḥ
verses.essence: Those who perform terrible austerities not ordained by scripture, yoked to hypocrisy and egoism, driven by the force of desire and passion —
verses.translation: Those men who practise terrific austerities not enjoined by the scriptures, given to hypocrisy and egoism, impelled by the force of lust and attachment.

anvaya.meaning: joined with the force of desire and passion

verses.explanation: Now the dark twin of Arjuna's question: not the rule-less worship of simple faith, but ghora tapas — ferocious self-mortification outside all guidance, powered by dambha and ahaṅkāra. Austerity can be ego's most convincing disguise: the spectacular fast, the public ordeal, suffering performed as superiority. Intensity, the Gita warns, is not the same as depth.
verses.connection: Distinguishes 17.1's innocent faith from its counterfeit: violence dressed as discipline.
verses.reflection: Distrust spectacular self-denial today, in yourself first — intensity is not depth.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the chapter's psychology: faith itself is threefold, born of one's inner nature — a person is made of their faith; as one's faith is, so is one. The sattvic worship gods; the rajasic, spirits of power; the tamasic, ghosts and shades — and those who practice fierce austerity unordained by scripture, torturing the body's elements and Me within it, know them of demonic resolve.

verses.devanagari:
कर्षयन्तः शरीरस्थं भूतग्राममचेतसः ।
मां चैवान्तःशरीरस्थं तान्विद्ध्यासुरनिश्चयान्॥
verses.transliteration:
karṣayantaḥ śarīrasthaṃ bhūtagrāmamacetasaḥ
māṃ caivāntaḥśarīrasthaṃ tānviddhyāsuraniścayān
verses.essence: — senselessly torturing the assembled elements in the body, and me too, dwelling within the body: know them to be of demonic resolve.
verses.translation: Senseless, torturing all the elements in the body and Me also, Who dwell in the body, know thou these to be of demonical resolves.

anvaya.meaning: tormenting what dwells in the body
the host of elements — the mindless ones
and me as well, seated within the body
know them as of asuric resolve

verses.explanation: The verdict carries a startling tenderness toward flesh: the body's elements are a bhūta-grāma, a *village* of beings entrusted to one's care — and brutalizing it torments mām caiva, 'me as well,' the indweller of 15.15. The body is not the soul's enemy but God's address. Self-violence, however piously framed, is vandalism of a temple from the inside.
verses.connection: Extends 16.18's insight inward: harming one's own body is also striking the resident Lord.
verses.reflection: Treat the body as a village in your care today: feed it, rest it, and torment none of its citizens.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the chapter's psychology: faith itself is threefold, born of one's inner nature — a person is made of their faith; as one's faith is, so is one. The sattvic worship gods; the rajasic, spirits of power; the tamasic, ghosts and shades — and those who practice fierce austerity unordained by scripture, torturing the body's elements and Me within it, know them of demonic resolve.

verses.devanagari:
आहारस्त्वपि सर्वस्य त्रिविधो भवति प्रियः ।
यज्ञस्तपस्तथा दानं तेषां भेदमिमं शृणु॥
verses.transliteration:
āhārastvapi sarvasya trividho bhavati priyaḥ
yajñastapastathā dānaṃ teṣāṃ bhedamimaṃ śṛṇu
verses.essence: Even the food dear to each is of three kinds, as are sacrifice, austerity and charity. Hear now the distinction between them.
verses.translation: The food also which is dear to each is threefold, as also sacrifice, austerity and almsgiving. Hear thou the distinction of these.

anvaya.meaning: but even the food of everyone
is threefold, according to what is dear

verses.explanation: The analysis goes domestic: four arenas — food, worship, discipline, giving — each refracted into three. Note the diagnostic key: priyaḥ, what is *dear* to each. Not what you eat under duress but what you crave reveals the strand in charge. The Gita is about to read the soul from the dinner plate — the most everyday spiritual audit in scripture.
verses.connection: Table of contents for 17.8–22: the three gunas traced through plate, altar, practice, and purse.
verses.reflection: Let today's audit start at the plate: what is dear to me reveals which strand is ordering.
verses.background: Even the plate testifies: foods dear to each strand are named — the savory and substantial that promote life, vitality, and cheer for sattva; the bitter, burning, over-salted that bring pain and disease for rajas; the stale, tasteless, putrid, and impure for tamas.

verses.devanagari:
आयुःसत्त्वबलारोग्यसुखप्रीतिविवर्धनाः ।
रस्याः स्निग्धाः स्थिरा हृद्या आहाराः सात्त्विकप्रियाः॥
verses.transliteration:
āyuḥsattvabalārogyasukhaprītivivardhanāḥ
rasyāḥ snigdhāḥ sthirā hṛdyā āhārāḥ sāttvikapriyāḥ
verses.essence: Foods that increase life, vitality, strength, health, happiness and delight — savory, mild, substantial and agreeable to the heart — are dear to the sattvic.
verses.translation: The foods which increase life, purity, strength, health, joy and cheerfulness (good appetite), which are savoury and oleaginous, substantial and agreeable, are dear to the Sattvic (pure) people.

anvaya.meaning: such foods are dear to the sattvic

verses.explanation: Sattvic eating defined by its aftermath: six increases — lifespan, clarity, strength, health, ease, even prīti, simple delight. The food itself: rasya, full of natural flavor; snigdha, gently rich; sthira, sustaining; hṛdya, kind to the heart. Nothing puritan here — pleasure is on the list — only the question every appetite should be asked: what does this leave behind in me?
verses.connection: First plate: nourishment measured by what it builds, not how loudly it tastes.
verses.reflection: Choose one meal today by its aftermath — what it builds in you — and still let it delight.
verses.background: Even the plate testifies: foods dear to each strand are named — the savory and substantial that promote life, vitality, and cheer for sattva; the bitter, burning, over-salted that bring pain and disease for rajas; the stale, tasteless, putrid, and impure for tamas.

verses.devanagari:
कट्वम्ललवणात्युष्णतीक्ष्णरूक्षविदाहिनः ।
आहारा राजसस्येष्टा दुःखशोकामयप्रदाः॥
verses.transliteration:
kaṭvamlalavaṇātyuṣṇatīkṣṇarūkṣavidāhinaḥ
āhārā rājasasyeṣṭā duḥkhaśokāmayapradāḥ
verses.essence: Foods that are bitter, sour, salty, very hot, pungent, harsh and burning — causing pain, grief and sickness — are coveted by the rajasic.
verses.translation: The foods that are bitter, sour, saline, excessively hot, pungent, dry and burning, are liked by the Rajasic and are productive of pain, grief and disease.

anvaya.meaning: such foods are desired by the rajasic

verses.explanation: The rajasic plate: everything turned up — ati-uṣṇa, *excessively* hot, sharp, scouring. Stimulation mistaken for satisfaction: food chosen to jolt rather than nourish, because the restless strand needs each meal to be an event. The bill arrives in three installments: duḥkha now, śoka after, āmaya — illness — on credit.
verses.connection: Second plate: intensity-seeking at the table, and its deferred invoice.
verses.reflection: Notice the craving for jolt over nourishment once today — and turn one dial down from 'event' to 'meal.'
verses.background: Even the plate testifies: foods dear to each strand are named — the savory and substantial that promote life, vitality, and cheer for sattva; the bitter, burning, over-salted that bring pain and disease for rajas; the stale, tasteless, putrid, and impure for tamas.

verses.devanagari:
यातयामं गतरसं पूति पर्युषितं च यत् ।
उच्छिष्टमपि चामेध्यं भोजनं तामसप्रियम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yātayāmaṃ gatarasaṃ pūti paryuṣitaṃ ca yat
ucchiṣṭamapi cāmedhyaṃ bhojanaṃ tāmasapriyam
verses.essence: That which is stale, tasteless, foul-smelling, left overnight, the leavings of others and unfit for offering — such food is dear to the tamasic.
verses.translation: That which is state, tasteless, putrid, rotten, refuse and impure, is the food liked by the Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: such food is dear to the tamasic

verses.explanation: The tamasic plate: yāta-yāmam, food whose hour has passed — life-force departed, only bulk remaining. The deeper note is indifference: eating as mere stuffing, without care for freshness, source, or sanctity (amedhyam — unfit even to offer). Where rajas over-attends to food and sattva attends rightly, tamas has stopped attending at all.
verses.connection: Third plate completes the food-mirror: appetite as a window into care itself.
verses.reflection: Refuse the food of indifference today — the stale, the careless — attention is the first ingredient.
verses.background: Even the plate testifies: foods dear to each strand are named — the savory and substantial that promote life, vitality, and cheer for sattva; the bitter, burning, over-salted that bring pain and disease for rajas; the stale, tasteless, putrid, and impure for tamas.

verses.devanagari:
अफलाङ्क्षिभिर्यज्ञो विधिदृष्टो य इज्यते ।
यष्टव्यमेवेति मनः समाधाय स सात्त्विकः॥
verses.transliteration:
aphalāṅkṣibhiryajño vidhidṛṣṭo ya ijyate
yaṣṭavyameveti manaḥ samādhāya sa sāttvikaḥ
verses.essence: Sacrifice offered according to rule by those desiring no fruit, with mind composed in the conviction 'offering is simply to be made' — that is sattvic.
verses.translation: That sacrifice which is offered by men without desire for reward as enjoined by the ordinance (scripture), with a firm faith that to do so is a duty, is Sattvic or pure.

anvaya.meaning: by those not hankering for fruit, sacrifice
'it is simply to be offered' — thus the mind

verses.explanation: Sattvic worship in one phrase: yaṣṭavyam eva — 'this is to be offered,' full stop. No transaction pending, no invoice attached; the act complete in itself, like a sunrise. Mind samādhāya, gathered and settled. This is the niṣkāma-karma of chapter 2 at the altar: worship as pure expression, not negotiation.
verses.connection: Worship enters the triage: the sattvic offering asks nothing back.
verses.reflection: Do one act today with the clean grammar: it is simply to be offered — no invoice attached.
verses.background: Sacrifice is graded by its grammar: offered as duty without desire for fruit, by a mind held in 'it is simply to be offered' — sattvic; performed for reward and display — rajasic; devoid of rule, food-offering, mantra, gift, and faith — tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
अभिसन्धाय तु फलं दम्भार्थमपि चैव यत् ।
इज्यते भरतश्रेष्ठ तं यज्ञं विद्धि राजसम्॥
verses.transliteration:
abhisandhāya tu phalaṃ dambhārthamapi caiva yat
ijyate bharataśreṣṭha taṃ yajñaṃ viddhi rājasam
verses.essence: But that which is offered with the fruit in view, and for the sake of display — know that sacrifice, best of Bharatas, to be rajasic.
verses.translation: The sacrifice which is offered, O Arjuna, seeking a reward and for ostentation, know thou that to be a Rajasic Yajna.

anvaya.meaning: but having the fruit in view
and even for the sake of show
which is offered, O best of Bharatas

verses.explanation: Two motives, often braided: abhisandhāya phalam, the worship-as-investment, divine favor expected on schedule; and dambhārtham, the ritual as theater, performed toward the human audience as much as heaven. The act may be liturgically perfect — the strand is read from the *aim*, never the choreography.
verses.connection: Rajasic worship: the altar as marketplace and stage at once — echoing 16.17's name-only rites.
verses.reflection: Catch worship performing for two audiences today — heaven and the neighbors — and dismiss one.
verses.background: Sacrifice is graded by its grammar: offered as duty without desire for fruit, by a mind held in 'it is simply to be offered' — sattvic; performed for reward and display — rajasic; devoid of rule, food-offering, mantra, gift, and faith — tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
विधिहीनमसृष्टान्नं मन्त्रहीनमदक्षिणम् ।
श्रद्धाविरहितं यज्ञं तामसं परिचक्षते॥
verses.transliteration:
vidhihīnamasṛṣṭānnaṃ mantrahīnamadakṣiṇam
śraddhāvirahitaṃ yajñaṃ tāmasaṃ paricakṣate
verses.essence: Sacrifice devoid of rule, without food offered, without mantra, without gifts, empty of faith — that they declare tamasic.
verses.translation: They declare that sacrifice to be Tamasic which is contrary to the ordinances of the scriptures, in which no food is distributed, which is devoid of Mantras, gifts and faith.

anvaya.meaning: devoid of rule, with no food distributed

verses.explanation: Tamasic worship defined by five absences: no order, no shared food, no sacred word, no honorarium to the officiant, and the void at the center — śraddhā-virahitam, faith itself missing. Ritual as empty husk, gone through because it was always gone through. The form survives the spirit's departure the way a shell outlasts the snail.
verses.connection: Completes the worship-triad: from selfless offering, to transaction, to hollow motion.
verses.reflection: Find any hollow ritual you still run — motions outliving meaning — and either refill it or retire it.
verses.background: Sacrifice is graded by its grammar: offered as duty without desire for fruit, by a mind held in 'it is simply to be offered' — sattvic; performed for reward and display — rajasic; devoid of rule, food-offering, mantra, gift, and faith — tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
देवद्विजगुरुप्राज्ञपूजनं शौचमार्जवम् ।
ब्रह्मचर्यमहिंसा च शारीरं तप उच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
devadvijaguruprājñapūjanaṃ śaucamārjavam
brahmacaryamahiṃsā ca śārīraṃ tapa ucyate
verses.essence: Worship of the gods, the twice-born, teachers and the wise; purity, uprightness, continence and harmlessness — this is called austerity of the body.
verses.translation: Worship of the gods, the twice-born, the teachers and the wise, purity, straightforwardness, celibacy and non-injury are called the austerities of the body.

anvaya.meaning: of gods, twice-born, teachers, the wise
is called austerity of the body

verses.explanation: The redefinition of tapas begins — and the body's austerity contains no ordeal at all: reverence rightly placed, cleanliness, uprightness, continence, harmlessness. After 17.5's self-torturers, the correction is pointed: true discipline of the body is not what you inflict on it but how it carries you through the world — whom it bows to, what it refrains from harming.
verses.connection: First panel of the tapas-triptych (17.14–16): austerity rebuilt as conduct, not ordeal.
verses.reflection: Practice body-tapas the true way today: bow rightly, stay clean, harm nothing — no ordeal required.
verses.background: Austerity is given its true anatomy in three registers: of the body — worship of gods, the twice-born, teachers, and the wise; purity, uprightness, continence, harmlessness; of speech — words that agitate none, true, pleasant, beneficial, and the practice of recitation; of mind — serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of motive. Practiced with supreme faith and no desire for fruit, it is sattvic; for honor and show, rajasic; with self-torture or to ruin another, tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
अनुद्वेगकरं वाक्यं सत्यं प्रियहितं च यत् ।
स्वाध्यायाभ्यसनं चैव वाङ्मयं तप उच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
anudvegakaraṃ vākyaṃ satyaṃ priyahitaṃ ca yat
svādhyāyābhyasanaṃ caiva vāṅmayaṃ tapa ucyate
verses.essence: Speech that causes no agitation, truthful, pleasant and beneficial; and the regular practice of sacred recitation — this is called austerity of speech.
verses.translation: Speech which causes no excitement, truthful, pleasant and beneficial, the practice of the study of the Vedas, are called austerity of speech.

anvaya.meaning: is called the austerity of speech

verses.explanation: Perhaps the most practical verse in the Gita: four tests for every sentence — does it disturb? is it true? is it kind? does it help? — anudvega-karam, satyam, priyam, hitam. Truth alone is not enough; truth weaponized fails the discipline. To pass all four filters, day after day, is a tapas more demanding than any fast — and svādhyāya keeps the tongue in training on sacred text. Anudvega-karam vakyam — speech that agitates no one, true, pleasant, beneficial: the four gates of vak-tapas, the austerity of the tongue. The commentaries note the conjunction is 'and', not 'or' — truth alone does not pass; truth weaponized fails the discipline. Svadhyayabhyasanam: and the tongue kept in training on sacred recitation. Gandhi called this verse his editorial policy; monastic rules across traditions independently rediscovered its four tests.
verses.connection: Second panel: the fiercest austerity relocated to the smallest muscle.
verses.reflection: Pass every sentence through the four gates today: does it disturb? is it true? kind? helpful?
verses.background: Austerity is given its true anatomy in three registers: of the body — worship of gods, the twice-born, teachers, and the wise; purity, uprightness, continence, harmlessness; of speech — words that agitate none, true, pleasant, beneficial, and the practice of recitation; of mind — serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of motive. Practiced with supreme faith and no desire for fruit, it is sattvic; for honor and show, rajasic; with self-torture or to ruin another, tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
मनः प्रसादः सौम्यत्वं मौनमात्मविनिग्रहः ।
भावसंशुद्धिरित्येतत्तपो मानसमुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
manaḥ prasādaḥ saumyatvaṃ maunamātmavinigrahaḥ
bhāvasaṃśuddhirityetattapo mānasamucyate
verses.essence: Serenity of mind, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of feeling — this is called austerity of the mind.
verses.translation: Serenity of mind, good-heartedness, self-control, purity of nature this is called mental austerity.

anvaya.meaning: is called austerity of the mind

verses.explanation: The innermost panel: manaḥ-prasāda, a mind kept clear like settled water; saumyatvam, moonlike mildness; mauna — not merely a closed mouth but the quiet *behind* thought; and the summit, bhāva-saṁśuddhi, the scrubbing of motive itself. The triptych completes a ladder inward: easy to police the hands, harder the tongue, hardest the silent intention no one else will ever audit.
verses.connection: Third panel: tapas at its root — where only the practitioner and the indweller can inspect.
verses.reflection: Take the inmost austerity for an hour: a settled mind, a mild manner, and one motive scrubbed clean.
verses.background: Austerity is given its true anatomy in three registers: of the body — worship of gods, the twice-born, teachers, and the wise; purity, uprightness, continence, harmlessness; of speech — words that agitate none, true, pleasant, beneficial, and the practice of recitation; of mind — serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of motive. Practiced with supreme faith and no desire for fruit, it is sattvic; for honor and show, rajasic; with self-torture or to ruin another, tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
श्रद्धया परया तप्तं तपस्तत्त्रिविधं नरैः ।
अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिर्युक्तैः सात्त्विकं परिचक्षते॥
verses.transliteration:
śraddhayā parayā taptaṃ tapastattrividhaṃ naraiḥ
aphalākāṅkṣibhiryuktaiḥ sāttvikaṃ paricakṣate
verses.essence: This threefold austerity, practiced with supreme faith by those disciplined and desiring no fruit — that they call sattvic.
verses.translation: This threefold austerity, practised by steadfast men, with the utmost faith, desiring no reward, they call Sattvic.
verses.explanation: The grading rubric applied to tapas: the full triptych — body, speech, mind — practiced śraddhayā parayā, with the highest faith, and signed with the now-familiar signature: aphalākāṅkṣibhiḥ, no fruit demanded. Discipline as devotion's posture rather than ambition's tool. The same fast, the same silence, can be any of the three strands; the faith and the aim decide.
verses.connection: Grades 17.14–16: the threefold practice turned sattvic by faith without bargain.
verses.reflection: Practice all three quietly today — body, speech, mind — wanting nothing back, and call it sattvic.
verses.background: Austerity is given its true anatomy in three registers: of the body — worship of gods, the twice-born, teachers, and the wise; purity, uprightness, continence, harmlessness; of speech — words that agitate none, true, pleasant, beneficial, and the practice of recitation; of mind — serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of motive. Practiced with supreme faith and no desire for fruit, it is sattvic; for honor and show, rajasic; with self-torture or to ruin another, tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
सत्कारमानपूजार्थं तपो दम्भेन चैव यत् ।
क्रियते तदिह प्रोक्तं राजसं चलमध्रुवम्॥
verses.transliteration:
satkāramānapūjārthaṃ tapo dambhena caiva yat
kriyate tadiha proktaṃ rājasaṃ calamadhruvam
verses.essence: Austerity practiced for honor, respect and reverence, and with ostentation — that is called rajasic here: unstable and impermanent.
verses.translation: The austerity which is practised with the object of gaining good reception, honour and worship, and with hypocrisy, is here said to be Rajasic, unstable and transitory.

anvaya.meaning: for the sake of welcome, honor, reverence

verses.explanation: Rajasic tapas: discipline performed toward an audience — satkāra, māna, pūjā: the welcome, the title, the deference. Its verdict is structural, not moral: calam adhruvam, unstable — because practice powered by applause stops when the applause does. What is done to be seen lasts exactly as long as the seeing.
verses.connection: The performative twin of 17.12's showy sacrifice: discipline with a gallery.
verses.reflection: Notice where your discipline performs for applause — and do that practice once today in secret.
verses.background: Austerity is given its true anatomy in three registers: of the body — worship of gods, the twice-born, teachers, and the wise; purity, uprightness, continence, harmlessness; of speech — words that agitate none, true, pleasant, beneficial, and the practice of recitation; of mind — serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of motive. Practiced with supreme faith and no desire for fruit, it is sattvic; for honor and show, rajasic; with self-torture or to ruin another, tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
मूढग्राहेणात्मनो यत्पीडया क्रियते तपः ।
परस्योत्सादनार्थं वा तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
mūḍhagrāheṇātmano yatpīḍayā kriyate tapaḥ
parasyotsādanārthaṃ vā tattāmasamudāhṛtam
verses.essence: Austerity practiced with deluded obstinacy, with self-torture, or for the sake of destroying another — that is declared tamasic.
verses.translation: That austerity which is practised out of a foolish notion, with self-torture, or for the purpose of destroying another, is declared to be Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: or for the ruin of another

verses.explanation: The darkest tapas: mūḍha-grāheṇa, gripped by a deluded fixation — discipline as self-punishment, or worse, parasyotsādanārtham, austerity wielded as a weapon, power accumulated to destroy. The epics are full of such figures: ascetics whose mountains of practice fund a single curse. Spiritual force, the verse warns, takes the moral shape of its wielder.
verses.connection: Closes the tapas-triad where 17.5–6 began: the ordeal that wounds self or other.
verses.reflection: Check that no practice of yours punishes you or aims at another — force is not the fruit.
verses.background: Austerity is given its true anatomy in three registers: of the body — worship of gods, the twice-born, teachers, and the wise; purity, uprightness, continence, harmlessness; of speech — words that agitate none, true, pleasant, beneficial, and the practice of recitation; of mind — serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of motive. Practiced with supreme faith and no desire for fruit, it is sattvic; for honor and show, rajasic; with self-torture or to ruin another, tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
दातव्यमिति यद्दानं दीयतेऽनुपकारिणे ।
देशे काले च पात्रे च तद्दानं सात्त्विकं स्मृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
dātavyamiti yaddānaṃ dīyate'nupakāriṇe
deśe kāle ca pātre ca taddānaṃ sāttvikaṃ smṛtam
verses.essence: The gift given with the thought 'it is simply to be given' — to one who can make no return, in fit place and time, to a worthy person — that gift is held to be sattvic.
verses.translation: That gift which is given to one who does nothing in return, knowing it to be a duty to give in a fit place and time to a worthy person, that gift is held to be Sattvic.

anvaya.meaning: 'it is to be given' — the gift thus given
given to one who cannot repay
in (right) place, time, and to a fit recipient

verses.explanation: The final arena: giving. Sattvic charity carries the chapter's signature phrase — dātavyam iti, 'it is to be given,' duty's clean grammar — and one piercing qualifier: anupakāriṇe, to one who *cannot reciprocate*. The gift escapes the economy entirely; no return possible, none imagined. Then the craft: right place, right time, right hands — generosity with its eyes open.
verses.connection: The purse examined: sattvic giving as the exit from exchange itself.
verses.reflection: Give once today where no return is possible — anupakarine — right time, right place, open hand.
verses.background: Giving, too, carries its maker's grade: the gift given as plain duty, at right place and time, to one who cannot repay — sattvic; given grudgingly or to get in return — rajasic; given at wrong place and time, to the unworthy, without respect or with contempt — tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
यत्तु प्रत्युपकारार्थं फलमुद्दिश्य वा पुनः ।
दीयते च परिक्लिष्टं तद्दानं राजसं स्मृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yattu pratyupakārārthaṃ phalamuddiśya vā punaḥ
dīyate ca parikliṣṭaṃ taddānaṃ rājasaṃ smṛtam
verses.essence: But that which is given expecting a return, or with the fruit in view, or given grudgingly — that gift is held to be rajasic.
verses.translation: And, that gift which is given with a view to receive something in return, or looking for a reward, or reluctantly, is held to be Rajasic.

anvaya.meaning: but that which is for a return-favor
and is given grudgingly, with strain

verses.explanation: Rajasic giving: the gift as opening move — pratyupakāra, the favor that books a favor; or merit banked toward heaven's ledger; or parikliṣṭam, the rupee released with a wince. The hand opens but the fist remains inside it. Such giving binds giver and receiver in a web of accounts where sattvic giving had cut both free.
verses.connection: The transactional middle: generosity still inside the marketplace of 17.12.
verses.reflection: Catch the gift with a hook in it today — the favor that books a favor — and unhook one.
verses.background: Giving, too, carries its maker's grade: the gift given as plain duty, at right place and time, to one who cannot repay — sattvic; given grudgingly or to get in return — rajasic; given at wrong place and time, to the unworthy, without respect or with contempt — tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
अदेशकाले यद्दानमपात्रेभ्यश्च दीयते ।
असत्कृतमवज्ञातं तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
adeśakāle yaddānamapātrebhyaśca dīyate
asatkṛtamavajñātaṃ tattāmasamudāhṛtam
verses.essence: The gift given at the wrong place and time, to unworthy recipients, without respect or with contempt — that is declared tamasic.
verses.translation: The gift that is given at a wrong place and time, to unworthy persons, without respect or with insult is declared to be Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: at wrong place and time, the gift

verses.explanation: Tamasic giving wounds while it gives: wrong moment, wrong hands, and the manner that poisons the matter — asatkṛtam avajñātam, tossed without respect, given *down*. A coin flung with contempt takes more than it leaves. The triad complete, the lesson across all three: in giving, the how and the why outweigh the what.
verses.connection: Completes the four-arena audit (food, worship, tapas, dana) — and 17.23 turns to the syllables that purify all of them.
verses.reflection: Mind the manner of one giving today: the how can wound or bless more than the what.
verses.background: Giving, too, carries its maker's grade: the gift given as plain duty, at right place and time, to one who cannot repay — sattvic; given grudgingly or to get in return — rajasic; given at wrong place and time, to the unworthy, without respect or with contempt — tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
ॐतत्सदिति निर्देशो ब्रह्मणस्त्रिविधः स्मृतः ।
ब्राह्मणास्तेन वेदाश्च यज्ञाश्च विहिताः पुरा॥
verses.transliteration:
OMtatsaditi nirdeśo brahmaṇastrividhaḥ smṛtaḥ
brāhmaṇāstena vedāśca yajñāśca vihitāḥ purā
verses.essence: 'OM TAT SAT' — this is recorded as the threefold designation of Brahman. By this were ordained of old the brahmanas, the Vedas, and the sacrifices.
verses.translation: "Om Tat Sat": This has been declared to be the triple designation of Brahman. By that were created formerly, the Brahmanas, the Vedas and the sacrifices.

anvaya.meaning: by that, the brahmanas and the Vedas
and the sacrifices were ordained of old

verses.explanation: After all the grading, the remedy: three syllables that consecrate. OM — the primal sound; TAT — 'That,' the pointing beyond; SAT — 'the Real, the Good.' The triple name of Brahman, older than the rites themselves, offered now as a seal: whatever imperfection clings to our mixed-strand offerings, these sounds re-address the package to the Absolute.
verses.connection: Opens the chapter's coda (17.23–28): the mantra that lifts imperfect acts toward the Real.
verses.schools_note: OM TAT SAT: the schools unfold the triple name with relish — Shankara as designation of Brahman through its scriptural, transcendent, and real aspects; Ramanuja as the consecrating formula linking all rites to the Lord; later teachers as the mantra that repairs imperfect action. The verse's quiet claim is shared: words themselves, rightly used, are instruments of purification.
verses.reflection: Seal one act today with the three syllables — OM TAT SAT — and feel it re-addressed to the Real.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the repair-formula: OM TAT SAT, the threefold designation of Brahman — OM beginning every Vedic act, TAT releasing acts from self-interest, SAT naming reality, goodness, and steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity, and gift. And the verdict on the missing ingredient: whatever is offered, given, or practiced without faith is asat — nothing here, nothing hereafter.

verses.devanagari:
तस्मादोमित्युदाहृत्य यज्ञदानतपःक्रियाः ।
प्रवर्तन्ते विधानोक्ताः सततं ब्रह्मवादिनाम्॥
verses.transliteration:
tasmādomityudāhṛtya yajñadānatapaḥkriyāḥ
pravartante vidhānoktāḥ satataṃ brahmavādinām
verses.essence: Therefore, with the utterance of 'OM', the acts of sacrifice, charity and austerity prescribed by scripture are always begun by the expounders of Brahman.
verses.translation: Therefore, with the utterance of "Om" are the acts of sacrifice, gift and austerity as enjoined in the scriptures, always begun by the students of Brahman.

anvaya.meaning: the acts of sacrifice, charity, austerity
always, of the expounders of Brahman

verses.explanation: OM in practice: the opening syllable, sounded before every sacred act like a key turned before a journey. Its function is orientational — one breath that gathers the scattered doer and points the deed toward its source. Millennia of practice stand behind the instruction: begin in the sound, and the act begins in the sacred.
verses.connection: First syllable applied: OM as the doorway every consecrated act walks through.
verses.reflection: Begin one undertaking today with OM — a single breath that points the deed at its source.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the repair-formula: OM TAT SAT, the threefold designation of Brahman — OM beginning every Vedic act, TAT releasing acts from self-interest, SAT naming reality, goodness, and steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity, and gift. And the verdict on the missing ingredient: whatever is offered, given, or practiced without faith is asat — nothing here, nothing hereafter.

verses.devanagari:
तदित्यनभिसन्धाय फलं यज्ञतपःक्रियाः ।
दानक्रियाश्च विविधाः क्रियन्ते मोक्षकाङ्क्षिभिः॥
verses.transliteration:
tadityanabhisandhāya phalaṃ yajñatapaḥkriyāḥ
dānakriyāśca vividhāḥ kriyante mokṣakāṅkṣibhiḥ
verses.essence: With 'TAT' — without aiming at the fruit — the acts of sacrifice and austerity and the manifold acts of charity are performed by the seekers of liberation.
verses.translation: Uttering ï1Tatï1, without aiming at the fruits, are the acts of sacrifice and austerity and the various acts of gifts performed by the seekers of liberation.

anvaya.meaning: fruit — the acts of sacrifice and austerity
and the manifold acts of giving
are performed by those who long for liberation

verses.explanation: TAT — 'That' — the most self-effacing word in language: pure reference away from oneself. Acting in its spirit, the mokṣa-kāṅkṣī releases the fruit at the moment of acting: this deed belongs to *That*, not to my account. One syllable performing the whole renunciation of 2.47 — the gift dissolved into the boundless the instant it leaves the hand.
verses.connection: Second syllable: TAT as the act's release into the Absolute — niskama karma in a single word.
verses.reflection: Say TAT inwardly as you finish one task: this belongs to That — and release it from your ledger.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the repair-formula: OM TAT SAT, the threefold designation of Brahman — OM beginning every Vedic act, TAT releasing acts from self-interest, SAT naming reality, goodness, and steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity, and gift. And the verdict on the missing ingredient: whatever is offered, given, or practiced without faith is asat — nothing here, nothing hereafter.

verses.devanagari:
सद्भावे साधुभावे च सदित्येतत्प्रयुज्यते ।
प्रशस्ते कर्मणि तथा सच्छब्दः पार्थ युज्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
sadbhāve sādhubhāve ca sadityetatprayujyate
praśaste karmaṇi tathā sacchabdaḥ pārtha yujyate
verses.essence: 'SAT' is used in the sense of reality and in the sense of goodness; likewise, Partha, the word 'SAT' is applied to any praiseworthy act.
verses.translation: The word ï1Satï1 is used in the sense of reality and of goodness; and so also, O Arjuna, the word ï1Satï1 is used in the sense of an auspicious act.

anvaya.meaning: in the sense of being-real, and being-good
the word 'SAT', O Partha, is used

verses.explanation: SAT carries a double load no translation holds at once: the *real* (sad-bhāva) and the *good* (sādhu-bhāva) — one word insisting that being and goodness are finally the same fact. Whatever is praiseworthy partakes of reality; evil, by this grammar, is a kind of unrealness. To act well is to be more real.
verses.connection: Third syllable unpacked: the metaphysics hidden in calling something 'sat'.
verses.reflection: Practice SAT both ways today: be more real by doing one unarguably good thing.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the repair-formula: OM TAT SAT, the threefold designation of Brahman — OM beginning every Vedic act, TAT releasing acts from self-interest, SAT naming reality, goodness, and steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity, and gift. And the verdict on the missing ingredient: whatever is offered, given, or practiced without faith is asat — nothing here, nothing hereafter.

verses.devanagari:
यज्ञे तपसि दाने च स्थितिः सदिति चोच्यते ।
कर्म चैव तदर्थीयं सदित्येवाभिधीयते॥
verses.transliteration:
yajñe tapasi dāne ca sthitiḥ saditi cocyate
karma caiva tadarthīyaṃ sadityevābhidhīyate
verses.essence: Steadfastness in sacrifice, in austerity and in charity is also called 'SAT'; and action for the sake of That is likewise designated 'SAT'.
verses.translation: Steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity and gift, is also called 'Sat' and also action in connection with these (or for the sake of the Supreme) is called 'Sat'.

anvaya.meaning: in sacrifice, in austerity, and in giving
and action done for the sake of That

verses.explanation: SAT extended from word to way of life: sthiti, the *staying* — showing up for the discipline again this week, this year — is itself the Real in action; and tad-arthīyam, anything whatsoever done for That's sake, earns the name. The sacred stops being a category of special acts and becomes a direction any act can face.
verses.connection: SAT made practical: constancy and God-ward intent as reality's own signature on a life.
verses.reflection: Let constancy be your offering today — showing up again is itself called SAT.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the repair-formula: OM TAT SAT, the threefold designation of Brahman — OM beginning every Vedic act, TAT releasing acts from self-interest, SAT naming reality, goodness, and steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity, and gift. And the verdict on the missing ingredient: whatever is offered, given, or practiced without faith is asat — nothing here, nothing hereafter.

verses.devanagari:
अश्रद्धया हुतं दत्तं तपस्तप्तं कृतं च यत् ।
असदित्युच्यते पार्थ न च तत्प्रेत्य नो इह॥
verses.transliteration:
aśraddhayā hutaṃ dattaṃ tapastaptaṃ kṛtaṃ ca yat
asadityucyate pārtha na ca tatprepya no iha
verses.essence: Whatever is offered, given or practiced without faith is called 'asat', Partha — it is nothing hereafter, and nothing here.
verses.translation: Whatever is sacrificed, given or performed, and whatever austerity is practised without faith, it is called 'Asat', O Arjuna; it is naught here or hereafter (after death).

anvaya.meaning: without faith — what is offered, given

anvaya.word: न च तत् प्रेत्य नो इह
anvaya.meaning: and it is naught hereafter, nor here

verses.explanation: The chapter's last word answers its first question by inversion: Arjuna asked about faith without scripture; the close addresses *works* without faith — and the verdict is annihilating: asat, unreal. The grandest sacrifice, performed hollow, na ca tat pretya no iha — counts nowhere, in no world. The whole chapter compresses to this: śraddhā is the substance; everything else is the container. Ashraddhaya hutam dattam — without faith, the offered, the given, the practiced: all of it asat, unreal — na cha tat pretya no iha, nothing hereafter, nothing here. The commentaries read the verse as the chapter's verdict delivered in accounting language: shraddha is the substance; act, scale, and form are containers. A torrent of faithless religion sums to zero on both ledgers, while 9.26's single leaf, loved, counts in full.
verses.connection: Seals the faith-chapter: forms without faith are nothing — the perfect threshold to ch18's question of renouncing forms themselves.
verses.reflection: Check the substance, not the container, tonight: whatever lacked faith today counted nowhere — refill it.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the repair-formula: OM TAT SAT, the threefold designation of Brahman — OM beginning every Vedic act, TAT releasing acts from self-interest, SAT naming reality, goodness, and steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity, and gift. And the verdict on the missing ingredient: whatever is offered, given, or practiced without faith is asat — nothing here, nothing hereafter.
